sentences.text: What about this one?
This is when we were already married.
I just found it here.
It saved me dragging down--
Oh, you've got the others there.
OK.
Now, who else hasn't got a glass?
My name is Maurice Kochan, Maurice, M-A-U-R-I-C-E.
My Christian name is K-O-C-H-A-N.
My surname, sorry.
Ko-han.
Yes, the 10th of April, 1927.
Beg you's?
And where were you born, the city and the country?
I'm born in Wadowice.
In Poland.
That's right.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I-- I remember when the war started.
Before?
Yes, all I can remember is that I attended school, and I attend a Jewish school, as well.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I used to enjoy sport.
And I used to go to a club, which calls Maccabi.
And I had a very good childhood.
I got-- had lovely parents.
And I enjoyed my childhood.
I had three brothers and four sisters.
I don't.
The first, I don't remember.
But I do remember being at school and playing soccer and playing all kind of things.
That I do remember.
It was a public school.
It was in the city, in Wadowice.
It wasn't a Jewish school, no.
There were quite a few Jewish children, yes.
Not-- not at school.
But I do remember antisemitism in Poland.
Yes.
Well, as I said, we were seven children.
And we were, I must say, all very, very good children.
And we had lovely parents, who enjoyed, especially, Friday nights.
Because my parents were not fanatics, but they were religion people.
They-- my father used to go to Saturday, and I used to come with him to shul every Saturday and on Friday night.
And--
Oh, I'm sure, my brothers went too.
But I always remember going with my father, walking, yes.
But my brothers went.
They all went to shul.
They-- on days that we went to shul.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Polish, yes.
No.
No, very seldom.
And we couldn't speak Yiddish.
I learned Yiddish, to speak, after the war.
We lived not far from the-- from the German border, that was southwest of Germany, not far from the German border.
Wadowice.
Not far from the German border, because we had another city, Bielsko, which was not far from us.
And there, it was the border.
Yes.
Cracow.
Yes.
Well, I had lots of friends at school.
Not only Jewish people, I had lots of-- lots of friends, Polish people.
And I enjoyed my school, very, very much.
Oh, yes.
We used to-- they used to come to my place.
I used to go to their place, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes, they did.
We had quite Polish friends that they used to come to our place, yes.
So they led a fairly open sort of--
Yeah, because Wadowice didn't have as many Jews.
There were not many Jews in our city.
Maybe a couple of thousand Jews.
But not many.
I'm not even sure if there were 2,000 families by then.
We were not a big Jewish community.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes, I remember going to cheder.
I remember, like today, I was pulling a-- still a little toy when I started going to cheder.
And I-- I was-- was going there for a little while.
And then I was quite a naughty boy, so my father brought the teacher home and he used to teach me at home.
[LAUGHS] Well, you know, how it used to be in my time.
We used to tear-- [LAUGHS] tear all the buttons from our things.
We used to play with it.
And-- and I didn't behave the way my parents would like me behave, so they brought the teacher home, and I used to-- till the end, till the war came, I had this teacher.
Very much, yes.
Very much, yes.
I even remember his name.
Yes, it was Alster.
Yeah.
Well, that's from the Jewish thing.
The--
Well, in-- in-- it might be a week or so before the war, when they started talking about it.
I-- I remember that the panic that it was coming on the city.
And then, I-- I remember when it started.
Well, I knew there was going to be a war, but I didn't understand too much about it.
Well, they did talk about it, because they were thinking what they're going to do if the war would come.
And matter of fact, when the war started, it was on a Friday night.
And on a Saturday morning, my father was trying to arrange a-- though he was religious, but he was trying to arrange a car, because he wanted to leave, not to be not so close to the border.
And he wanted to get away, further away from it.
So he rented a car.
And we were all there, waiting, and my mother disappeared.
And we didn't know where mum was.
And what she did-- see my father understood.
He was a religious person.
So was my mother, but my father, religiously, he knew more than-- than my mother did.
And he-- she-- what she did, she went to the rabbi, in the city, to ask him if he would allow her to go, on a Saturday, by car?
So he said, if I would have a car, I would go, too.
And so she came, and she went with us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And tell me, just before we go on with that-- and I want to get on with that-- can you tell me a bit more about your brothers and sisters?
Yes.
One-- one brother-- What, from '39?
Well, I--
Well, I--
I was the youngest in the family.
I really don't remember too much about them.
I really don't.
So I don't like to say something that I really don't remember it.
All I can say, I-- I was the baby.
Well, I was very secure.
I was very secure, because we were quite well off, you know.
And I was secure in every way.
I wasn't missing on anything when I was a child.
I always could get a few-- there were zlotys in Poland.
Well, I-- I always had a few zlotys with me, to go to school, to buy things.
So I never missed anything as a child.
And I was the youngest.
I was spoiled.
Not really.
I played with the other children, outside, not with my brothers and sisters.
The youngest sister, yes, but not with the others anymore.
Well, we had a menswear-- a menswear store and a liquor restaurant.
They were two separate business, yes.
Yes, they were involved in it, yes.
There were two brothers, yes, and there was an elder sister, as well, the eldest sister.
But the brothers were involved in it, more than anything, with the parents.
Well, on the-- on the first night, when the war broke out, as I said before, we tried to get a car to get away from the city.
And we were very, very lucky, because, on the Saturday morning, the restaurant that we had, if it wouldn't be a Saturday, some of the family would be dead today.
But they would die then, because the first bomb that fell in our city was on our restaurant.
So we were lucky, because Saturday it was closed.
We didn't open the shop on a Saturday.
So that was closed.
And the people that they lived there were killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we went by car.
We went to Dzialoszyce--
--to a a different city.
100 and-- about 120-- roughly 120 kilometers.
Dzialoszyce.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we arrived to Dzialoszyce, we rent a little house.
It only had a kitchen and a room.
And we were seven children, where we came from three bedrooms, which in Poland was quite a-- a comfortable house.
And we were all living in one-- one bedroom and a kitchen place.
So we were-- some of them, at night, we were on the floors, just having a bed made-up.
And we all slept together.
No.
No, I wasn't frightened, because, at the time, we haven't seen the Germans yet, there.
It took a while, a few days, by the time the Germans arrived.
And I even remember when the Germans arrived.
I went to look, seen the tank coming in.
You know, they were talking.
We didn't realize what-- what's going to happen.
Nobody knew anything there, you know?
I remember, even like today, there-- one of the ladies was saying that they're-- they're lovely people.
They're nice people.
No, we really didn't realize what-- what ahead of us.
Well, in Poland, there was always antisemitism.
It was always antisemitism.
But me being so young, I maybe didn't take so much notice.
But there was, because I remember always calling names on Jews, you know, and things.
But in Poland, they were very much antisemitic, because I must say that I was afraid more of the Poles then I was afraid of the Germans, because the Germans wouldn't recognize you as a Jew.
They couldn't say that you are definitely a Jew.
And the Poles, he looked at you, and he could point out, right away, that you were Jewish.
Not really, no.
No, not really.
No, but the-- and it was quite big antisemitism in Poland.
Well, in '39, as soon as the Germans came in, as they walked into Dzialoszyce, the first thing they did, after a few days, close all the schools for Jewish children.
There were no schools for Jewish children.
And this is the only thing that I'm missing, because all I could finish was five classes.
There wasn't a Jewish school, no.
But Jews were not allowed to go.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
And I was just, you know?
Maybe as a child, I was happy that I didn't go to school.
I don't know.
I don't remember those things.
But maybe so.
But things started to get, from one day-- worse from one day to the next.
And it was really just terrible.
You know, more Germans came into the city.
And we didn't know what to do, because we had the home in Wadowice.
And we had now a home in Dzialoszyce.
So when the Germans already arrived, my parents decided that my mother and three sisters would go back to Wadowice and look after the home in Wadowice.
And we would stay here.
And we see, maybe, it won't take too long, so we all go back together.
But unfortunately, it didn't happen.
So the-- it wasn't for much-- yeah, but what-- what happened is the-- the worry my father had, because we couldn't receive any mail from them.
And he started to worry terribly that what happened to them.
Because there was no mail going there.
And all we knew that they arrived all right home.
But a week went by, two weeks, three weeks, and we didn't have any mail from them.
So they were really-- he was really worrying.
And-- and I could see my father, what he was going through.
And I suggest, once, to my father, that, if he would buy me a bicycle, I would go.
Because I'm the youngest, and the Germans wouldn't do anything to a-- a young boy like this.
So he didn't want to hear about it.
And my other brother, which was with us too, he-- he wouldn't go.
He was-- he was-- he was afraid.
He realized, more than me, that it's not so easy.
But I loved bicycles.
I used to love-- I had a bicycle, always.
And I loved the bicycle.
And my father wouldn't be interested in it.
But then another couple weeks went by, and he didn't hear anything.
And I begged him, because I could see really what he was-- how he was suffering.
[CRYING]
So, [SOBBING] and he decided that he would buy me a bicycle.
And he bought me a bicycle.
And I got up at 4:00 in the morning, because I told my father that I want to get up very early, because I was terribly scared at night.
I was terribly scared at night.
And I didn't know how long it will take me, because I had to 220 kilometers.
So I got up very early in the morning, and I went.
My father was crying, and I went.
[SOBBING] I was 12 years of age, yes.
[CRYING] On the way, I was-- I was doing quite all right.
And Germans were, by the thousands, passing me.
And no one stopped me at all.
I was just doing all right.
I was about 15 kilometers from home or 20 kilometers from home, I can't remember exactly.
And I was the happiest person, because I still had plenty of time.
Because I was afraid it will get dark.
So I had plenty of time.
But then, as I came closer, someone was shouting, this boy on the bicycle is a Jew-- a Pole.
So I was really scared that the Germans will hear something, and I could be really in trouble.
But I was going so fast.
And I arrived home.
When I arrived home, I didn't even enter the home, and we had a dog, and he was barking like he would know that I'm coming.
And when they opened the door for me, he jumped on me.
I remember like today.
You know, he hasn't seen us for quite a long time.
And my mother was there and the three sisters.
So I had a shower.
And my mother gave me something to eat.
I think I told her that I can't stay, because she wanted me to stay a few days.
I said, no, because father's worrying terribly, and I have to go first thing in the morning.
I have to go straight back to give him the  news.
So my mother said, how can you do it in-- in two days?
You know, you shouldn't do it.
No, but I didn't.
I said, no, I promised my father that I would be back as soon as possible.
And in the morning, I got up very early again, and my mother made me breakfast.
And I went.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the way, right through, I had a terrific ride.
I had a much better ride than I had the first time, because I knew already, and no one stopped me or anything.
It was really beautiful.
And I arrived-- and when I was close to home, I was the happiest person.
Because I did it for my father, what he wanted me to do.
And when I came home, [SOBBING] I have never seen my father-- he just took me in his hands, and he was crying.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] And I was really happy that I could do this for him.
That?
Yes.
I did 240-- 240 kilometers.
Well, I left very early in the morning.
I left about, say, between 4 and 5 o'clock in the morning, because that's the one thing I wanted to do, because I was afraid, very much, at night.
I wouldn't go from here to there at night.
So-- and this is what I did.
And I had no problem at all. 120 kilometers I made a day on the bicycle.
Oh, it was bombed.
The-- the restaurant was bombed.
And the menswear, when we took a car-- we had a big car-- and we took as much as we could.
We took out, to Dzialoszyce, everything from the menswear store, whatever we could.
But we couldn't take everything.
So we left the things.
But the-- when the Germans came in, they took everything.
The Germans.
Yes.
When they-- yes.
The first-- the first bomb fell in Wadowice on this-- on our restaurant.
And three people were killed there.
Well, when I came back to my father, after-- after a while, then it took about another couple weeks or three weeks, and then mail started to-- to arrive.
And we were very happy, because then even we could send them parcels.
And we were quite happy.
We were only hoping that the war won't last too long, and we'd be able to go back to our home.
Unfortunately, it didn't happen so.
And then they were trying-- you see, Wadowice belonged once to Germany in the First World War.
And they were trying to-- the Germans wanted to make a border there.
So when we heard this, we wanted mum and-- and her-- the sisters to leave there, to come to Dzialoszyce.
But we didn't realize that the border, they will make it so fast.
Unfortunately, the did it.
And-- and they was-- they were staying there, for a while, because we had to make arrangements, how to bring them to Dzialoszyce.
So there was somebody in-- in Wadowice that would take us through the border, for a lot of money, which we did.
And after a while, they managed to cross the border, all of them.
And they were very lucky, because there were no Jews, anymore, in the city anywhere.
The Jews were-- the Jews were in ghettos or they were in the concentration camps or whatever.
Not from our family.
Not from our-- but why I'm saying about the ghetto, because, when-- when my sister and-- and my mother went to Cracow, well, to start with, they had to wear a-- a yellow-- a star of David.
And they didn't, because they-- they couldn't travel on a train.
And that's what they did.
So they were just risking their lives.
And in Cracow, a policeman stopped them, a Polish policeman.
He said, what are you looking at?
Because that was the ghetto there.
And they said, nothing.
We're just looking at things.
And he just let them go.
And then they came to Dzialoszyce.
And we were the happiest people in the world, because the whole family was together again.
So we were very happy.
We were not comfortable, but-- but everybody was happy.
It doesn't matter where we slept or so, but we were all together.
The only one person who wasn't with us was the oldest brother, because he was in the army.
But it didn't take long, and he came home, as well.
They let him go.
He was a prisoner of war.
And he came back, so we were the whole family, and we were happy.
But unfortunately, we were happy, but, every day, it was getting worse.
And it was impossible to live, the way the Germans were going on with things.
Well, every day, there are new orders.
They-- I don't know if you heard, there was a Judenrat, which-- a Jewish committee.
And the Germans used to give them orders, what to do.
Today, for example, they want a kilo of gold.
And if not, they will kill so and so many Jews.
And-- and they used to work, they-- they ask every day.
And things were getting impossible to live.
And then they stopped you from going outside the city.
You were not allowed to go outside the city at all.
Jews were not allowed to go.
But we took a risk.
My-- I must say, that in-- my brothers, none of them looked Jewish or the sisters.
And we-- they didn't worry.
They risked.
And because, for menswear-- for menswear, what we had in Dzialoszyce, for one suit, you could get a lot of food.
And they used to go outside the city and bring chickens or bread or anything, milk, everything for that.
So we had-- really, we didn't suffer with the hunger at all.
So they traded the clothes--
And we traded, yes.
And we knew--
--lots of Poles, yes.
That they were happy to do it, because you couldn't find during-- after the-- the war a suit or something like that.
We had beautiful things, because, from the shop, we took everything.
So we really didn't know-- didn't know what hunger means, because we had plenty to eat.
And I'm sure lots of people suffered a terrible lot, because there was no food around in the city.
And unfortunately, as the time was getting on, it was getting so bad that it was just impossible to live anymore.
And they were killing people and taking people to concentration camps.
And we-- we heard that people were taken to Belzec or things.
We found out that they were gassed.
And also, people were worrying, really, that, you know, what will happen to us?
When did you find that out?
What year was this?
I think it was in '42, already.
In '42, yes.
The year after that, we left.
You see, when-- a few days before, we heard that the Germans are coming in.
And they're going to liquidate the whole city, and all the Jews will go.
And no one knew what will happen.
So when we heard this, we were trying to do something about it.
And that was signed before we were selling to the Poles the suits, and we had quite acquaintance with them.
And one of the Poles, when we told him what's going to happen, he said, look, don't worry.
When something will happen, you just come, and you stay at our place.
So we will look after you.
We've-- I've got a hiding, and you will be at our place there.
So we were very happy.
And we were discussing, with our parents, this.
But my parents didn't want to go.
So we were really worrying, because we could see that every day it was getting worse.
And we knew that they could come any day now, the Germans.
So what we did-- We-- we might stop here.
All right.
